data.title: Ponce Financial Group Inc expected to post earnings of 17 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Ponce Financial Group Inc is expected to show a rise in quarterly revenue when it reports results on July 28 (estimated) for the period ending June 30 2025

  • The Bronx New York-based company is expected to report a 24.0% increase in revenue to $22.2 million from $17.9 million a year ago, according to the estimate from one analyst, based on LSEG data.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Ponce Financial Group Inc is for earnings of 17 cents per share.

  • The one available analyst rating on the shares is "strong buy".

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Ponce Financial Group Inc is $15.00, about 8.5% above its last closing price of $13.72

data.title: Brazil’s Consumer Confidence Edges Higher in July
data.description:
The seasonally adjusted FGV-IBRE Consumer Confidence Index rose by 0.8 points to 86.7 in July 2025, reversing the previous month’s decline and continuing the upward trend that began in March.

The modest increase reflected slight improvements in both current conditions (+0.5 to 83.4) and expectations (+0.7 to 89.4), mainly among lower-income households.

Among the components, assessments of the economy (+0.7 to 94.3) and household finances (+0.3 to 72.8) advanced.

However, future outlooks showed mixed results: while the indicator for durable goods purchases surged (+10.8 to 86.9), expectations for the economy (-2.4 to 100.5) and household finances (-6.2 to 82.4) deteriorated.

According to FGV-IBRE economist Anna Carolina Gouveia, the data suggests a gradual recovery in confidence, supported by current improvements but still constrained by uncertainty regarding the future.

data.title: NCS Multistage Holdings Inc expected to post a loss of $1.29 a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• NCS Multistage Holdings Inc is expected to show a fall in quarterly revenue when it reports results on July 31 for the period ending June 30 2025

  • The Houston Texas-based company is expected to report a 6.6% decrease in revenue to $27.74 million from $29.69 million a year ago, according to the mean estimate from 2 analysts, based on LSEG data.The company's guidance on May 1 2025, for the period ended June 30, was for revenue between $26.00 million and $29.00 million.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for NCS Multistage Holdings Inc is for a loss of $1.29 per share.

  • The one available analyst rating on the shares is "strong buy".

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for NCS Multistage Holdings Inc is $38.50, about 20.2% above its last closing price of $30.73

The company's guidance on May 1 2025 for the period ended June 30 was for Earnings before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation and Amortization of between 26 million and 0 (USD).The company's guidance on May 1 2025 for the period ended June 30 was for gross profit margin between USD37% and USD39%.

This summary was machine generated July 25 at 11:49 GMT. All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. (For questions concerning the data in this report, contact Estimates.Support@lseg.com. For any other questions or feedback, contact RefinitivNewsSupport@thomsonreuters.com)

data.title: SNG: South Africa: Airbus opens new support hub in Johannesburg
data.description:
Staff Writer

Airbus has opened a new customer support centre for commercial aircraft in Johannesburg, reinforcing its long-standing partnership with Africa’s aviation sector. The centre marks a key milestone in the company’s nearly 50-year presence on the continent.

The Johannesburg facility will offer technical assistance, engineering and maintenance solutions, fleet performance analysis, training services, and on-site support for all Airbus commercial aircraft families, including the A220, A320, A330, and A350.

“The new centre expands Airbus’ presence in Africa and underscores our confidence in the region’s potential, as we invest in local capabilities, empower our customers, drive connectivity and shared progress across the continent,” said Gabriel Semelas, president of Airbus in the Middle East and Africa.

Supporting African airline operations

By strengthening customer proximity, the new centre is designed to help African airlines operate safe, efficient, and reliable fleets. Airbus currently serves nearly 40 African carriers operating more than 260 Airbus aircraft.

According to the company’s Global Services Forecast, Africa will require 14,000 new pilots and 21,000 maintenance professionals over the next 20 years to support projected growth in air travel demand.

Broader presence across the continent

Airbus has been active in Africa since the first A300 aircraft delivery in 1976. Its broader African footprint includes Airbus Helicopters, which this year marks 30 years of operations in Southern Africa, where its Midrand hub provides maintenance, spare parts, and the continent’s first H125 virtual reality simulator for pilot training.

Airbus Defence and Space also provides support to African governments through military aircraft, Earth observation services, and satellite-based connectivity.

With more than 180 African suppliers integrated into its global supply chain, Airbus contributes to local job creation, skills development, and industrial growth across the continent.

data.title: Biogen Gets CHMP Backing for Zurzuvae in Postpartum Depression
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Biogen said a key European regulatory committee has recommended approval of its Zurzuvae treatment for women with postpartum depression.

Biogen on Friday said a green light would make Zurzuvae the first drug authorized in the European Union specifically indicated to treat depressive symptoms for women with the mood disorder.

The European Commission, which generally follows CHMP's advice, will now review the recommendation, with a decision expected in the third quarter, the Cambridge, Mass., biotechnology company said.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved Zurzuvae, a once-daily 14-day treatment, for postpartum depression in 2023.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: Gold Futures Fall as Risk-On Sentiment Dulls Safe-Haven Appeal — Market Talk
data.description:
1144 GMT - Gold futures slip as risk-on market sentiment prevails and the dollar strengthens. Futures are down 0.9% at $3,342.40 a troy ounce. The precious metal is under pressure on fading safe-haven demand after the U.S.-Japan trade deal, Tradu.com's Nikos Tzabouras says in a note. The deal is expected to reduce the hit from President Trump's tariff policies on both the global and U.S. economies, Tzabouras writes. This optimism, coupled with strong U.S. jobs data, has lowered hopes for imminent Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. While this is positive for the dollar, it has weighed on noninterest bearing bullion, Tzabouras says. That said, gold's safe-haven appeal could quickly return on trade tensions ahead of the Aug. 1 tariff-reimplementation deadline and central bank demand remains supportive, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1136 GMT - While European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde this week seemed to downplay odds of an interest-rate cut at its next meeting in September, waiting might put the bank behind the curve, Oxford Economics' Oliver Rakau says in a note. "We think the next two inflation readings are likely to undershoot their call," he says. A stronger euro will also weigh on exports and inflation later this year, he says. With a 15% U.S. tariff rate seeming to be the new floor, there are further downside risks to growth even if uncertainty fades after a deal. That could make the ECB late to react as the economic fallout of higher tariffs unfolds, Rakau says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1127 GMT - The Turkish lira's recent losses look set to continue after Turkey's central bank cut interest rates by 300 basis points to 43% on Thursday, Commerzbank's Tatha Ghose says in a note. This rate cut looks premature, he says. The cooling of annual inflation in June reflects misleading base effects that might end soon, he says. Underlying inflation remains elevated. The risk of inflation rising in the near-term lingers due to higher retail gas prices and lira depreciation, he says. If inflation fails to ease as the central bank expects, its credibility "will go for a tailspin." The dollar falls 0.3% to 40.5529 lira after hitting a four-month high of 40.6830 overnight, LSEG data show.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1111 GMT - The Russian ruble remains weaker against the dollar after Russia's central bank cut interest rates by 200 basis points to 18.00% as widely anticipated. The central bank said inflationary pressures are falling faster than previously anticipated while domestic demand growth is slowing. It said monetary conditions will remain as tight as necessary to return inflation to the 4% target in 2026. This implies an average key rate in the range of 18.8% to 19.6% per annum this year and 12.0% to 13.0% per annum next year. It expects inflation to return to 4% in 2026 and remain at the target thereon. The dollar rises 0.1% to 79.3500 rubles, little changed from levels before the decision. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1014 GMT - Another policy rate cut by the European Central Bank this year shouldn't dent the earnings of the continent's banks, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note after the central bank held rates steady at 2%. The market pencils in another cut by the end of the year though this seems far from certain given the ECB's reassuring and balanced press conference, analysts write. "As banks increase their [net interest income] resilience, each 25 basis point parallel shift is worth just 1% of sector earnings," they say. KBW continues to see the upward sloping yield curve as an important offset for bank earnings. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0958 GMT - Investors could be reluctant to sell dollars as part of the month-end rebalancing of portfolios, Barclays analysts say in a note. The bank's month-end rebalancing model indicates weak dollar selling against major currencies by month-end. Elevated core inflation, resilient economic activity and a robust labor market boosted the dollar in the first half of July. However, these gains were later fully offset by President Trump's repeated pressure on Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell to cut interest rates. The positive momentum in U.S. equities extended while U.S. bonds underperformed. The bank measures the performance of foreign versus U.S. bonds and equities to indicate month-end forex demand from investors who want to maintain protection against losses stemming from swings in currencies. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - Singapore's manufacturing output growth is likely to moderate in 2H, OCBC chief economist Selena Ling says in a note. The city-state's manufacturing output was off to a strong start in 1H, rising 8.2% from a year earlier as companies were rushing shipments before U.S. tariffs kick in Aug. 1. While Singapore's tariff rate isn't likely to deviate from the initial 10% level announced on Liberation Day, potential tariffs on the semiconductor and pharmaceutical sectors point to some payback risk in 2H, Ling says. A lack of concessions could pressure Singapore's manufacturing growth. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0914 GMT - China's campaign to tackle price wars and overcapacity in the economy may take longer to show results compared with supply-side reform undertaken over 2015-2018 due to a lack of demand-side policy support, DBS analysts write in a note. China has stepped up efforts to regulate disorderly price competition and reduce excess capacity. This has boosted market sentiment over Chinese and Hong Kong equities, but the impact on economic fundamentals may take longer to materialize. Weaker incentives for local governments to stimulate demand due to fiscal constraints may slow progress, the bank says. Addressing issues in downstream sectors such as EVs and batteries via government policies may take longer to take effect compared with at state-owned enterprises, DBS says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0913 GMT - Bitcoin falls to a two-week low as investors take profits after the cryptocurrency's recent rally. Data from CoinGlass show investors closing earlier bets on bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies rising. Some $383 million in long positions have been closed in the past 24 hours with bitcoin accounting for $141 million of the total, the data show. It follows recent gains in cryptocurrencies after the U.S. passed three crypto bills on regulation for the industry last week. Bitcoin falls to a low of $114,779, having reached a record high of $123,153 last Monday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0843 GMT - Treasury yields rise after U.S. data Thursday showed improvements in the economy. U.S. interest-rate cuts over the coming two months look less likely as a result. U.S. weekly jobless claims fell while U.S. purchasing managers' surveys showed the composite activity measure reached a seven-month high of 54.6. "With that strong data in hand, investors dialed back the likelihood of Federal Reserve rate cuts," in turn lifting Treasury yields, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Increases in yields are limited, however, as President Trump continues to call for the central bank to lower rates. The 10-year Treasury yield rises nearly 1 basis point on the day to 4.416%, albeit staying below Thursday's intraday peak of 4.442%, Tradeweb data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0842 GMT - The euro falls against the dollar after a key survey showed German business sentiment improved by less than expected in July. The ifo business climate index rose to 88.6 in July from 88.4 in June. Economists in a WSJ survey expected the index to rise to 89.0. The business expectations gauge increased to 90.7 in July from 90.6 in June, also below the 91.2 forecast. The euro falls to $1.1741 after the data, from $1.1752 beforehand. The 10-year German Bund yield is last up 6 basis points to 2.760%, little changed from levels before the data, Tradeweb data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0837 GMT - The Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone straddling Singapore and Malaysia is attracting interest from beyond the region, says Ng Wei Wei, chief executive of United Overseas Bank Malaysia in an emailed interview. Many multinational corporations with established regional hubs in Singapore view the project as a strategic step for expansion, Ng says. Beyond Singapore, UOB is seeing active engagement from companies headquartered in China, Japan and South Korea. "More recently, we have noted growing interest from the western bloc, including Germany, France and the United States," Ng says. She adds that companies are closely monitoring the regulatory framework to come, especially around cross-border governance and long-term policy alignment. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

This rate cut looks premature, he says. The cooling of annual inflation in June reflects misleading base effects that might end soon, he says. Underlying inflation remains elevated. The risk of inflation rising in the near-term lingers due to higher retail gas prices and lira depreciation, he says. If inflation fails to ease as the central bank expects, its credibility "will go for a tailspin." The dollar falls 0.3% to 40.5529 lira after hitting a four-month high of 40.6830 overnight, LSEG data show.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1111 GMT - The Russian ruble remains weaker against the dollar after Russia's central bank cut interest rates by 200 basis points to 18.00% as widely anticipated. The central bank said inflationary pressures are falling faster than previously anticipated while domestic demand growth is slowing. It said monetary conditions will remain as tight as necessary to return inflation to the 4% target in 2026. This implies an average key rate in the range of 18.8% to 19.6% per annum this year and 12.0% to 13.0% per annum next year. It expects inflation to return to 4% in 2026 and remain at the target thereon. The dollar rises 0.1% to 79.3500 rubles, little changed from levels before the decision. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1014 GMT - Another policy rate cut by the European Central Bank this year shouldn't dent the earnings of the continent's banks, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note after the central bank held rates steady at 2%. The market pencils in another cut by the end of the year though this seems far from certain given the ECB's reassuring and balanced press conference, analysts write. "As banks increase their [net interest income] resilience, each 25 basis point parallel shift is worth just 1% of sector earnings," they say. KBW continues to see the upward sloping yield curve as an important offset for bank earnings. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0958 GMT - Investors could be reluctant to sell dollars as part of the month-end rebalancing of portfolios, Barclays analysts say in a note. The bank's month-end rebalancing model indicates weak dollar selling against major currencies by month-end. Elevated core inflation, resilient economic activity and a robust labor market boosted the dollar in the first half of July. However, these gains were later fully offset by President Trump's repeated pressure on Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell to cut interest rates. The positive momentum in U.S. equities extended while U.S. bonds underperformed. The bank measures the performance of foreign versus U.S. bonds and equities to indicate month-end forex demand from investors who want to maintain protection against losses stemming from swings in currencies. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0952 GMT - Singapore's manufacturing output growth is likely to moderate in 2H, OCBC chief economist Selena Ling says in a note. The city-state's manufacturing output was off to a strong start in 1H, rising 8.2% from a year earlier as companies were rushing shipments before U.S. tariffs kick in Aug. 1. While Singapore's tariff rate isn't likely to deviate from the initial 10% level announced on Liberation Day, potential tariffs on the semiconductor and pharmaceutical sectors point to some payback risk in 2H, Ling says. A lack of concessions could pressure Singapore's manufacturing growth. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0914 GMT - China's campaign to tackle price wars and overcapacity in the economy may take longer to show results compared with supply-side reform undertaken over 2015-2018 due to a lack of demand-side policy support, DBS analysts write in a note. China has stepped up efforts to regulate disorderly price competition and reduce excess capacity. This has boosted market sentiment over Chinese and Hong Kong equities, but the impact on economic fundamentals may take longer to materialize. Weaker incentives for local governments to stimulate demand due to fiscal constraints may slow progress, the bank says. Addressing issues in downstream sectors such as EVs and batteries via government policies may take longer to take effect compared with at state-owned enterprises, DBS says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0913 GMT - Bitcoin falls to a two-week low as investors take profits after the cryptocurrency's recent rally. Data from CoinGlass show investors closing earlier bets on bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies rising. Some $383 million in long positions have been closed in the past 24 hours with bitcoin accounting for $141 million of the total, the data show. It follows recent gains in cryptocurrencies after the U.S. passed three crypto bills on regulation for the industry last week. Bitcoin falls to a low of $114,779, having reached a record high of $123,153 last Monday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0843 GMT - Treasury yields rise after U.S. data Thursday showed improvements in the economy. U.S. interest-rate cuts over the coming two months look less likely as a result. U.S. weekly jobless claims fell while U.S. purchasing managers' surveys showed the composite activity measure reached a seven-month high of 54.6. "With that strong data in hand, investors dialed back the likelihood of Federal Reserve rate cuts," in turn lifting Treasury yields, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Increases in yields are limited, however, as President Trump continues to call for the central bank to lower rates. The 10-year Treasury yield rises nearly 1 basis point on the day to 4.416%, albeit staying below Thursday's intraday peak of 4.442%, Tradeweb data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0842 GMT - The euro falls against the dollar after a key survey showed German business sentiment improved by less than expected in July. The ifo business climate index rose to 88.6 in July from 88.4 in June. Economists in a WSJ survey expected the index to rise to 89.0. The business expectations gauge increased to 90.7 in July from 90.6 in June, also below the 91.2 forecast. The euro falls to $1.1741 after the data, from $1.1752 beforehand. The 10-year German Bund yield is last up 6 basis points to 2.760%, little changed from levels before the data, Tradeweb data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0837 GMT - The Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone straddling Singapore and Malaysia is attracting interest from beyond the region, says Ng Wei Wei, chief executive of United Overseas Bank Malaysia in an emailed interview. Many multinational corporations with established regional hubs in Singapore view the project as a strategic step for expansion, Ng says. Beyond Singapore, UOB is seeing active engagement from companies headquartered in China, Japan and South Korea. "More recently, we have noted growing interest from the western bloc, including Germany, France and the United States," Ng says. She adds that companies are closely monitoring the regulatory framework to come, especially around cross-border governance and long-term policy alignment. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Coursera Stock Soars 28%. The Online Learning Platform Jumps After Earnings Beat. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Coursera stock surged in premarket trading Friday after the online learning platform reported strong second-quarter adjusted profit and boosted its revenue guidance for the full year.

Shares rose 28% to $11.65, putting them on track for a new 52-week high and the highest close since July 2024, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Futures tracking the benchmark S&P 500 index were up slightly.

The steep gains came after Coursera reported adjusted earnings of 12 cents a share, above the 9 cents Wall Street had anticipated. Revenue came in at $187.1 million in the quarter, topping the $180.5 million consensus estimate among analysts polled by FactSet.

On a non-GAAP basis, the company posted a loss of 5 cents, narrower than the 15-cent loss reported in the same period last year.

Coursera raised its revenue outlook for the full year to a range of $738 million to $746 million, up from $720 million to $730 million previously.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

data.title: Wellgistics Health Converts $8.1M Debt to Equity
data.description:
TAMPA, FLORIDA / ACCESS Newswire / July 25, 2025 / Wellgistics Health, Inc. ("Wellgistics Health" or the "Company"), a leader in next-generation pharmaceutical distribution, digital prescription routing, and AI-powered hub fulfillment, today announced that its Board of Directors has approved an $8.1 million debt-to-equity conversion.

Led by CEO Brian Norton and former minority partners of Wellgistics, LLC, which was acquired by Wellgistics Health in 2024, the transaction strengthens the Company's balance sheet by materially reducing the Company's short-term debt.

The transaction is also expected to:

  • Accelerate the Company's cash flow by unlocking more favorable terms and capital access;

  • Improve Wellgistics Health's ability to acquire high-demand pharmaceutical products at competitive prices;

  • Expand the Company's national footprint by growing its network of independent pharmacies; and

  • Strengthen the Company's proprietary technology platform, including AI-powered hub services and digital prescription routing

"This conversion will make a significant contribution in enabling us to accelerate our business plan and accomplish the goals we believe will help transform the prescription drug marketplace and drive long-term value for our shareholders," said Wellgistics Health CFO Mark DiSiena.

"We look forward to sharing additional positive developments in the near future."

About Wellgistics Health

Wellgistics Health delivers medications from manufacturer to patient-faster, smarter, and more affordably. Its integrated platform connects 6,500+ independent pharmacies and 200+ U.S. manufacturers, offering wholesale distribution, digital script routing, and AI-driven hub services like eligibility, adherence, onboarding, prior authorization, and cash-pay fulfillment. As a PBM-agnostic alternative, Wellgistics Health delivers seamless, compliant, end-to-end solutions designed to restore access, transparency, and trust in U.S. healthcare.

data.title: The S&P 500 Is Grinding Out Record After Record — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

So far this week, the S&P 500 has ended every day at a new high.

The moves have been modest-on Thursday, the index advanced just 0.07%-but this is the longest streak of closing records since December, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Futures edged higher early Friday, suggesting the streak could continue.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Roche Says European Medicines Agency Issued Negative Opinion on Elevidys
data.description:
By Mauro Orru

Roche Holding said the European Medicines Agency wasn't recommending Elevidys for children aged three to seven with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, but that it would continue talking to the agency to find a solution.

The Swiss pharmaceutical group said the negative opinion from the European Medicines Agency was based on data that included a phase 3 study in which Elevidys didn't meet its primary endpoint after one year.

However, Roche said Elevidys showed significant improvements across key secondary endpoints when compared to a placebo, adding the company would continue its dialogue with the agency to explore how to make Elevidys available to patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy in the European Union.

Duchenne muscular dystrophy is a progressive condition that causes muscles to get weaker over time

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: Bristol Myers Names Massacesi Chief Medical Officer as Hirawat Departs
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Bristol Myers Squibb's chief medical officer, Samit Hirawat, is leaving the drugmaker to pursue new professional opportunities.

Bristol Myers on Friday said Cristian Massacesi, most recently chief medical officer and oncology chief development officer at AstraZeneca, will succeed Hirawat as executive vice president, chief medical officer and head of development on Aug. 1.

The Princeton, N.J., company said Hirawat, who joined as chief medical officer in 2019, will remain as an adviser through Nov. 1 to assist with the transition.

Bristol Myers said Massacesi will oversee its early- and late-stage product development across all therapeutic areas.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: Phio Pharmaceuticals Announces Agreement with U.S. Manufacturing Source for Drug Substance
data.description:
Agreement covers contract development and manufacturing of lead compound PH-762

King of Prussia, Pennsylvania--(Newsfile Corp. - July 25, 2025) - Phio Pharmaceuticals Corp. is a clinical-stage siRNA biopharmaceutical company developing therapeutics using its proprietary INTASYL® gene silencing technology to eliminate cancer. Phio announced today it has entered into a comprehensive drug substance development services agreement with a U.S. manufacturer. The company will provide analytical and process development and cGMP manufacture of Phio's lead clinical development compound PH-762. This represents a critical next step in advancing Phio's intratumoral program to treat cutaneous carcinomas. Phio is currently enrolling patients for the 5th and expected final cohort in its Phase 1b dose escalation study for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, Merkel cell carcinoma and melanoma.

"We are delighted to be partnering with an organization known for its quality and expertise in oligonucleotide chemistry sequencing," said Mr. Bitterman, Phio's President and CEO. "Additionally, we value the strategic advantages to our management team of working with a U.S. based organization."

About Phio Pharmaceuticals Corp.

Phio Pharmaceuticals Corp. is a clinical-stage siRNA biopharmaceutical company advancing its INTASYL® gene silencing technology focused on immuno-oncology therapeutics. Phio's INTASYL compounds are designed to enhance the body's immune cells to more effectively kill cancer cells. Phio's lead clinical program is an INTASYL compound, PH-762, that silences the PD-1 gene implicated in various forms of skin cancer. The on-going Phase 1b trial (NCT# 06014086) is evaluating PH-762 for the treatment of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma and Merkel cell carcinoma. PH-762 is a potential non-surgical treatment for skin cancers.

For additional information, visit the Company's website, www.phiopharma.com.

data.title: HCA raises annual profit forecast on resilient demand for medical care
data.description:
HCA Healthcare raised its 2025 profit forecast on Friday as it expects sustained demand for medical procedures to cushion a hit from U.S. President Donald Trump's potential tariffs.

The company expects 2025 profit per share to be about $25.50 to $27 per share, compared with its previous forecast of $24.05 to $25.85 per share.

data.title: Brazil's current account deficit reaches $5.131 billion in June
data.description:
Brazil posted a current account deficit of $5.131 billion in June, central bank data showed on Friday, while economists polled by Reuters were expecting a narrower $4.363 billion shortfall.

Foreign direct investment for the month totaled $2.81 billion, well below the $4.5 billion forecast in the poll.

data.title: JD’s German retail bid has oddly compelling logic
data.description:
The $53 bln Chinese e-commerce group may offer $2.6 bln for Düsseldorf-based Ceconomy, which runs electronics stores. The return isn’t bad – and may rise if JD boosts online sales. Diversifying away from a brutal home market also makes sense. Politics could be an issue, however.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Yawen Chen on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

German electronics retailer Ceconomy confirmed on July 24 that it was in advanced negotiations about a potential cash takeover by China’s JD.com, at 4.6 euros per share.

The retailer, which owns electronics chains MediaMarkt and Saturn, said that no legally binding agreements had yet been signed and it was not certain whether a deal would happen.

Ceconomy shares were trading at 3.75 euros when the German stock market closed on July 23. They rose 12% to 4.2 euros as of market close on July 24, and traded 1% higher at 4.3 euros as of 0730 GMT on July 25.
